content.text: We study which integers are squares modulo a prime.

content.title: Quadratic Residue
content.content:
Let $p$ be an odd prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$. We say $a$ is a 
**quadratic residue** (QR) modulo $p$ if the equation $x^2 \equiv a \pmod{p}$ 
has a solution. Otherwise, $a$ is a **quadratic nonresidue** (QNR).

content.title: Count of Quadratic Residues
content.content:
For an odd prime $p$, there are exactly $(p-1)/2$ quadratic residues 
and $(p-1)/2$ quadratic nonresidues modulo $p$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The map $x \mapsto x^2$ sends $\{1, 2, \ldots, p-1\}$ to the set of 
QRs. This map is 2-to-1: $x^2 = (-x)^2$, and if $x^2 \equiv y^2$ then 
$x \equiv \pm y$. Thus there are $(p-1)/2$ QRs.

content.title: Legendre Symbol
content.content:
For an odd prime $p$ and integer $a$, the **Legendre symbol** is:
\[
\left(\frac{a}{p}\right) = 
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } p \mid a \\
1 & \text{if } a \text{ is a QR mod } p \\
-1 & \text{if } a \text{ is a QNR mod } p
\end{cases}
\]

If $p \mid a$ or $p \mid b$, both sides are 0.

Otherwise, use primitive roots. If $g$ is a primitive root mod $p$, 
then $a \equiv g^i$ and $b \equiv g^j$ for some $i, j$. Then $a$ is 
a QR iff $i$ is even. Thus $\left(\frac{a}{p}\right) = (-1)^i$, 
$\left(\frac{b}{p}\right) = (-1)^j$, and 
$\left(\frac{ab}{p}\right) = (-1)^{i+j}$.

content.text:
This multiplicativity reduces computation to evaluating 
$\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)$ for primes.
